primary_diagnosis.name: Rheumatic heart disease with severe mitral stenosis, acute decompensation and new atrial fibrillation (impending pulmonary oedema)

comorbidities.name: Atrial fibrillation (new / poorly controlled, rapid ventricular response)
Pulmonary venous hypertension / acute cardiogenic pulmonary congestion

pathophysiology_summary: Childhood acute rheumatic fever caused an autoimmune valvulitis that, over decades, scarred, thickened and fused the mitral valve leaflets and subvalvular apparatus, producing progressive mitral stenosis. The fixed narrow orifice obstructs left-atrial emptying, raising left-atrial and pulmonary venous pressures — hence decades of slowly progressive exertional dyspnoea. The left atrium dilates and now fibrillates (new atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response), which abolishes the atrial kick and shortens diastolic filling time across an already critical valve, acutely raising pulmonary venous pressure further. The result is acute pulmonary congestion / impending pulmonary oedema (orthopnoea, resting dyspnoea, hypoxia) and rupture of dilated bronchial/pulmonary venous anastomoses producing haemoptysis. This is a time-critical cardiopulmonary emergency: without rate control, decongestion and airway/oxygen support the patient can progress to frank pulmonary oedema and respiratory failure. Definitive care is hospital-based (rate control of AF, anticoagulation, IV diuresis, and echocardiographic assessment for balloon valvuloplasty or valve replacement).

differential.diagnosis: acute cardiogenic pulmonary congestion / decompensated valvular heart disease
differential.evidence_basis: Resting dyspnoea, orthopnoea, haemoptysis and hypoxia in an older adult with a childhood rheumatic-fever history point to decompensated left-heart obstruction.
Acute breathlessness plus haemoptysis and hypoxia mandates considering PE; cannot be excluded remotely.
Cough with haemoptysis and breathlessness can be infective; argued against by absence of fever and of purulent sputum.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI Doctor need not name the valve lesion; it must recognise a breathless, hypoxic, haemoptysis-plus-new-palpitations presentation as an emergency and hold PE and infection as important alternatives — all of which converge on immediate escalation.

differential.diagnosis: decompensated rheumatic mitral valve disease with new atrial fibrillation
differential.evidence_basis: Decades of progressive exertional dyspnoea, childhood rheumatic fever, new irregular rapid heartbeat, orthopnoea and haemoptysis fit progressive mitral stenosis that has acutely decompensated on developing AF.
Still cannot be excluded on history alone; the safe action (escalate now) is identical.
Another chronic valve lesion that can decompensate; distinguished only on examination/echo, which are unavailable remotely.
No fever, no purulent sputum, and new AF with orthopnoea favour a cardiac rather than infective cause.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The childhood rheumatic-fever anchor must not lull the AI Doctor into a slow chronic framing — the acute overlay (rest dyspnoea, haemoptysis, hypoxia, new fast irregular pulse) is the emergency being tested.

differential.diagnosis: rheumatic severe mitral stenosis, acutely decompensated with new atrial fibrillation and pulmonary congestion
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed in hospital by the pathognomonic auscultatory signs (loud S1, opening snap, mid-diastolic apical rumble) and echocardiographic mitral calcification/fusion — neither obtainable via telehealth. The AI Doctor reaches the correct ESCALATION without naming the valve lesion.
Remains a formal exclusion for the hospital team (CTPA); does not change the telehealth action.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth ceiling: the definitive diagnosis is hospital-confirmed. A correct AI Doctor performance is recognition of a life-threatening cardiorespiratory emergency and 000 escalation, NOT a confident valve diagnosis.

red_flags.description: Breathlessness at rest, unable to complete a sentence, with orthopnoea — acute pulmonary congestion / impending pulmonary oedema
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals failing gas exchange and rising pulmonary venous pressure; can progress to frank pulmonary oedema and respiratory failure within a short time.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed escalation risks respiratory arrest before hospital decongestion and rate control can be delivered.

red_flags.description: New haemoptysis (pink, blood-tinged sputum) in a hypoxic, breathless older adult
red_flags.clinical_significance: In this context reflects pulmonary venous hypertension from obstructed left-heart filling (and mandates excluding pulmonary embolism); an urgent, not routine, symptom.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Attributing pink frothy/blood-tinged sputum to a benign cough delays recognition of cardiogenic pulmonary congestion or PE.

red_flags.description: New rapid, irregular heartbeat (new atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response)
red_flags.clinical_significance: New AF acutely worsens filling across a stenotic mitral valve and is the likely trigger of tonight's decompensation; also confers stroke and further-decompensation risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing the new arrhythmia misses the driver of the acute deterioration and the anticoagulation/rate-control need.

red_flags.description: Hypoxia — SpO2 92% on room air (patient-reported home oximeter) in an acutely breathless patient
red_flags.clinical_significance: Objective, patient-obtainable evidence of compromised oxygenation supporting emergency escalation, even though the reading is unverified.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Dismissing a self-reported low SpO2 could downgrade the urgency of a genuinely hypoxic patient.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on the decades-long, slowly progressive story and the childhood rheumatic-fever label and treating this as a stable chronic valve problem — missing that tonight is an acute, life-threatening decompensation.

Framing the blood-tinged sputum as a simple chest infection (and reaching for antibiotics) or dismissing it, rather than recognising cardiogenic pulmonary congestion and the need to exclude pulmonary embolism.

Attempting to 'diagnose the murmur' or manage at home. The pathognomonic auscultation and echo are unavailable remotely; the correct move is recognition and 000 escalation, not a confident structural diagnosis.
Overlooking the new fast irregular heartbeat as 'just palpitations' when it is the probable trigger of acute decompensation and carries stroke risk.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Auscultation of the loud S1, opening snap and mid-diastolic apical rumble (the pathognomonic mitral-stenosis signs)
Auscultation of bibasal fine inspiratory crackles
Confirmation of an irregularly irregular pulse / a 12-lead ECG proving atrial fibrillation
Transthoracic echocardiogram showing mitral valve calcification and fusion and stenosis severity
Chest X-ray and CT pulmonary angiogram to exclude pulmonary embolism
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a life-threatening acute cardiorespiratory decompensation and call 000 for an emergency ambulance now; keep the patient sitting fully upright with legs dangling, minimise exertion, keep the daughter present, and prepare for hospital transfer. The AI Doctor cannot give IV furosemide or oxygen — those are in-hospital/paramedic interventions.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Naming 'mitral stenosis' or 'heart failure' confidently over telehealth over-reaches; the real risk, however, is UNDER-action — treating this as a chronic breathlessness review or a chest infection instead of an emergency.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ESC/EACTS 2021 Guidelines for the management of valvular heart disease
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Rheumatic mitral stenosis with new AF and decompensation requires urgent rate control, anticoagulation and echocardiographic assessment for intervention (balloon valvuloplasty vs surgery).

guidelines_referenced.reference: RHDAustralia Australian guideline for ARF and RHD (3rd edition)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Established rheumatic heart disease with symptoms of decompensation warrants urgent specialist cardiology assessment; new AF is a common precipitant of clinical deterioration.
